unit.name: Supervised vs unsupervised learning and probabilistic modeling basics
unit.description: Sets up the lecture by separating learning paradigms from probabilistic model families and introducing how normalization creates probability-mass competition.
unit.summary: Before diving into specific generative models, the instructor notes that the big practical change over the past decade was scale—more compute, bigger datasets, and more stable training—rather than a wholesale rewrite of the underlying mathematics [ts=634s] [ts=701s]. He then distinguishes supervised learning, where data are paired as (x, y) and the goal is to learn a mapping from inputs to labels, from unsupervised learning, where only x is observed and the aim is to uncover structure such as clusters, low-dimensional manifolds, or density estimates [ts=745s] [ts=806s] [ts=865s]. The lecture next introduces the separate axis of discriminative versus generative modeling, emphasizing that probability densities are normalized and therefore induce competition for a fixed unit of probability mass [ts=952s] [ts=969s] [ts=1001s]. In that framing, discriminative models learn p(y|x) so labels compete for each fixed input and the model cannot reject unreasonable inputs, while generative models learn p(x), letting all possible images compete and enabling explicit low-probability rejection of out-of-scope inputs; conditional generative models instead learn p(x|y) and make images compete within each conditioning signal [ts=1052s] [ts=1105s] [ts=1138s] [ts=1239s].

key_points.text: Supervised learning uses paired data (x, y) to learn a mapping from inputs to targets.
Unsupervised learning uses only x and seeks hidden structure such as clustering, dimensionality reduction, or density estimation.
A normalized density assigns mass across possible outcomes, so different outcomes compete for a fixed unit of probability mass.
Discriminative models learn p(y|x), so labels compete for each image but different images do not compete with one another.
Generative models learn p(x), allowing the model to assign low probability to unreasonable inputs and therefore reject outliers.
Conditional generative models learn p(x|y), so all possible outputs compete within each conditioning signal y.
